coredata.dc:title: Enhanced visible light harvesting in dye-sensitized solar cells through incorporation of solution-processable silver plasmons and anthracite-derived graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rao, Akshatha A.
Narendhiran, Santhosh
Balachandran, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: The major setback for the enhanced performance of DSSC is the narrow absorption window and the interfacial exciton recombination. Therefore, in this work, the photovoltaic performance of dye-sensitized solar cells has been improved by the synergistic effect of anthracite-derived graphene quantum dots and silver plasmons. GQD and Ag coupled photoanodes were fabricated by a facile solution processable process under room temperature. The as-fabricated DSSC TiO2/Ag/GQD (TAG) exhibited an enhanced power conversion efficiency of 10.5 % with a current density of 22.40 mAcm−2 measured under solar irradiation of 100 mWcm−2 with AM 1.5G. An enhancement surpassing 30.5 % was obtained for the champion cell when compared to the pristine TiO2 based DSSC. Furthermore, this study emphasizes developing a cutting-edge approach for the high-quality use of fossil fuel-derived graphene quantum dots in energy conversion systems, thereby encouraging the green conversion of fossil fuels and broadening the potential of anthracite coal's utilization in energy conversion applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface plasmon resonance
Anthracite coal
Graphene quantum dots
Dye-sensitized solar cells
Linear sweep voltammetry
Open circuit voltage decay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles and preparation of TiO2 paste
Preparation of counter electrode and assembling of DSSC

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Staatssekretariat für Bildung, Forschung und Innovation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge DST for major research fund DST/TMD/SERI/ARSS/2020/37(G), Centre for Research Christ (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221. The authors also acknowledge the central instrumentation facility (CIF) Christ (Deemed to be University) for the instrument facility. We also thank DST-FIST for their support through the research fund SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). Authors are also grateful to Central Research Facilities, IIT Delhi, Sonipat campus for their support with XPS characterisation. Akshatha A Rao also acknowledges UGC for the research fund obtained from SJSGC Fellowship. Grant number: UGCES-22-GE-KAR-F-SJSGC-3769.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of T and TAG (b) Raman spectra of GQD showing the characteristic G and D bands, (c) and (d) Raman spectra of T and TAG in 100–1800 cm−1 range and 100-200 cm−1 range respectively.
(a) FESEM images of TAG film, (b) elemental mapping of the film showing the presence of C, Ag, O and Ti, (c) cross-sectional SEM image of bare photoanode (T) and modified photoanode (TAG).

(a) TEM micrograph at 100 nm magnification (Inset: shows the particle size distribution diagram) (b) GQDs with well-ordered crystal lattice (c) HRTEM micrograph showing the lattice parameter of the anthracite derived GQDs.
(a) UV–Visible absorbance and photoluminescence emission of GQDs (b) UV-DRS absorbance spectra and, (c) Reflectance plot of T, TA, TG and TAG films, respectively.
XPS analysis of T and TAG and their corresponding (a) survey scan spectrum of T (b) survey scan spectrum of TAG (c)Ti 2p (d) Ag 3d (e) O1s and (f) C1s core level spectra.
(a) Current Density vs Voltage curves (b) EQE plot, (c) Nyquist plot, (d) Bode plot of T, TG, TA and TAG based DSSCs respectively.
(a) PL spectra and (b) OCVD plot of T, TG, TA and TAG-based DSSCs, respectively.
(a) LSV plot and (b) Mott-Schottky plot of T, TG, TA and TAG-based DSSCs.
Schematic diagram showing an illustration of the Ag@GQD mechanism in DSSC.
The fabrication procedure of Ag@GQD based photoanode.

Photovoltaic parameters showing the current density, open circuit voltage, fill factor, efficiency and EIS parameters showing the series resistance and charge transfer resistance, respectively for the fabricated DSSC.

head.ce:title: Enhanced visible light harvesting in dye-sensitized solar cells through incorporation of solution-processable silver plasmons and anthracite-derived graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, 560029, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, 560029, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The major setback for the enhanced performance of DSSC is the narrow absorption window and the interfacial exciton recombination. Therefore, in this work, the photovoltaic performance of dye-sensitized solar cells has been improved by the synergistic effect of anthracite-derived graphene quantum dots and silver plasmons. GQD and Ag coupled photoanodes were fabricated by a facile solution processable process under room temperature. The as-fabricated DSSC TiO2/Ag/GQD (TAG) exhibited an enhanced power conversion efficiency of 10.5 % with a current density of 22.40 mAcm−2 measured under solar irradiation of 100 mWcm−2 with AM 1.5G. An enhancement surpassing 30.5 % was obtained for the champion cell when compared to the pristine TiO2 based DSSC. Furthermore, this study emphasizes developing a cutting-edge approach for the high-quality use of fossil fuel-derived graphene quantum dots in energy conversion systems, thereby encouraging the green conversion of fossil fuels and broadening the potential of anthracite coal's utilization in energy conversion applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy has emerged as a viable alternative to conventional energy sources, and dye-sensitized solar cells are promising technologies for converting sunlight into electricity with high efficiency and low fabrication costs [1]. Dye-sensitized solar cells are one of the most potentially useful technologies that have been used for converting incident photons directly into electricity with ease of fabrication. Photoanode, sensitizer, electrolyte, and counter electrode are the four components of a DSSC. Photoanode is the backbone of the solar cell as it aids as a transport path for the charges generated by the sensitizer [2,3]. Several metal oxide semiconductors that have been used as a photoanode material in dye-sensitized solar cells include TiO2 [4], SnO2 [5], ZnO [6], BaSnO3 [7,8] and WO3 [9]. Titanium dioxide is the most commonly used photoanode material in DSSCs. The major setbacks for the enhanced performance of DSSC are the exciton recombination at the photoanode-electrolyte interface, mainly caused by the structure and porosity of the photoanode [10], the optical properties associated with the material and the low utilization of red and NIR wavelength regions, which limit the light-harvesting efficiency at longer wavelength led to the quest for modifying the DSSC device [11]. These shortfalls could be addressed by reducing the exciton recombination that occurs at the interfaces by the process of surface engineering and by extending the spectral absorption window with the aid of co-sensitized technology.

Surface plasmon resonance effect (SPR) has been employed as a viable approach for enhancing the efficiency of DSSC. SPR is the collective oscillation of electrons present in the conduction band of a metal nanoparticle triggered by the electromagnetic field of the incident light, which has the potential to significantly boost the dye's light absorption and photon carrier production. However, the absorption range is limited to a shorter wavelength of the visible light. Metal nanoparticles derived from Silver (Ag) [4] and gold (Au) [11], copper (Cu) [12] and Aluminium (Al) [13] have attracted attention for their localized surface plasmon resonance effect.

Several studies were done by research groups (Dissanayake et al. [14], Brishty Deb Choudhury et al. [15] have shown an improvement in the cell's performance with the inclusion of metal nanoparticles in their photoanode framework. In the work, multi-shaped silver nanoparticles were synthesized using the chemical reduction method, and the coupled plasmonic effect was studied in DSSC by M. Kandasamy et al. [16]. The as-fabricated DSSC gave a maximum efficiency of 7.64 % for the TiO2-silver nanoparticles composite. In the study shown by Bhojana et al. [4] the TiO2–Ag nanocomposite based DSSC exhibited an overall efficiency of 5.24 %. Dong et al. [17] studies exhibited an overall efficiency of 8.43 % with the incorporation of the core-shell technique of Au@Ag TiO2-based DSSCs. The synthesis procedure followed mostly involves the synthesis of metal nanoparticles and, later, the preparation of composites and their use in the DSSC to employ the SPR effect. However, in this study, we used a facile solution processable technique to develop a plasmon-enriched TiO2 surface capped with graphene quantum dots.

On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-tiny fragments of graphene derived from various sources [18,19] show exceptional optoelectronic properties and have been interesting candidates for light emitting diodes [20], sensing [21], photocatalysis [22] and photovoltaic devices [23]. The optical properties of GQDs have been utilized and used as sensitizers [24] and co-sensitizers in DSSC [25,26], and its usage has aided in enhancing the absorption region and resulted in improved efficiency. However, little attention has been paid to their graphene-like properties. Graphene quantum dots have been derived from various carbonaceous sources that include graphite, graphene oxide, glucose, and carbon nanotubes [18,27–31]. The higher cost of production and the tedious chemical processes have limited the wider commercialization of these materials. Therefore, green synthesis routes to derive nanocarbons via facile methods have gained more interest.

An abundantly available and viable natural resource, Coal is also a source for the synthesis of nanocarbon. Crystalline carbon within the structure of coal is simple to displace by facile synthesis route, resulting in nanometre-sized carbon nanostructures with amorphous carbon addends on the edges. In addition to having a high carbon content and abundant crystalline carbon domains, coal makes a great source of nanocarbons due to its cost-effectiveness as well [32]. In this work, TiO2 (the most commonly used photoanode material because of its better surface area and stability [33]) is coupled with silver plasmons and graphene quantum dots to boost charge transfer and enhance light absorption. Silver is one of the viable elements for improving the performance of DSSC. Its surface plasmonic resonance aids in an increased optical absorption, thereby increasing the photocurrent [15]. By combining silver nanoparticles and GQD an enhancement in the absorbance was exhibited, which in turn aided in better current density and overall power conversion efficiency of the device.

Thus, for the first time, we have coupled the properties of silver plasmons with anthracite coal-derived GQDs and employed them to enhance the performance of DSSC. Due to their synergistic effect, an improved efficiency of 10.50 % with a current density of 22.40 mAcm−2 and an open circuit voltage of 0.76 V was observed. The champion cell, which was comprised of Ag and GQD, showed an enhancement surpassing 30.5 % in power conversion efficiency compared with the pristine TiO2-based DSSC.

The nanocarbon precursor used in the present study is Indian Anthracite. Collected samples were powdered to obtain micrometre-sized particles before treatment. Polyethylene glycol, Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP), Triton X-100, Sulfuric acid, N-dimethyl formamide, silver nitrate, polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) - were analytical grade products purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The cis-diisothiocyanato-bis (2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II) bis (tetrabutyl-ammonium) (N719), Platisol (T/SP), Iodolyte HI-30 were purchased from Solaronix SA, Switzerland. All the chemicals were used without any further purification.

GQDs were synthesized by the one-step solvothermal approach. Initially, anthracite was demineralized using H2SO4. 0.2 g of pre-treated coal was mixed in 40 ml of DMF. The mixture was sonicated, transferred into an autoclave, and treated for 12 h at 180 °C. The above solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm, and the supernatant was further dialyzed for 48 h in a 3.5 KDa dialysis membrane. The obtained GQDs were dried and stored for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles and preparation of TiO2 paste
ce:section.ce:para: The simple sol-gel technique was employed for the synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles, as reported by Santhosh et al. [34]. In brief, a known amount of Isopropyl alcohol polyethylene glycol (molecular weight = 20000) was stirred under warm conditions until the mixture was completely dissolved. Further, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was added to the above mixture. In the next step, titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP) was added dropwise under stirring conditions. The white gel formed was left undisturbed for the gelling process. After 24 h, the obtained gel was washed with water and ethanol via centrifugation several times. The white sediment was further dried at 120 °C for 8 h. After drying the TiO2 powder, it was sintered at 450 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace to improve the crystallinity of the as-synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles. Further, TiO2 paste was prepared by mixing TiO2 powder with ethanol and water in a 1:1 ratio. Triton X-100 was added dropwise at regular intervals, and the mixture was finely ground until a colloidal solution was obtained.

The FTO glass was cut into a dimension of 2 cm × 1.7 cm and washed thoroughly with DI water and ethanol to remove the contaminants on the FTO. A compact layer of titanium-bis-acetylacetonate di-isopropoxide was spin-coated, and later, the TiO2 paste was coated using the doctor blade technique. The coated cells were sintered at 125 °C, 375 °C and 500 °C to remove the binders used in the preparation of the TiO2 paste and to improve the crystallinity of the TiO2 coated. Further, the photoanodes were subjected to TiCl4 treatment and sintered at 500 °C for 30 min. The as-prepared photoanodes are named T. In addition, for the plasmonic-based photoanode, TiO2 photoanodes were fabricated by placing them in a solution mixture of AgNO3 and PVP and were exposed to UV radiation for 2 h and are named as (TiO2/Ag) TA as shown in Scheme 1 .

The prepared photoanodes were coated with GQDs and dried at 100 °C and the GQD added photoanodes are named as (TiO2/Ag/GQD) TAG. On the other hand, the TiO2 photoanodes were coated with GQD and are named as (TiO2/GQD) TG.
All the fabricated photoanodes (T, TG, TA and TAG) were dipped in 0.5 mM of N719 dye dissolved in acetonitrile and tert-butanol (1:1 vol ratio) and were kept for 24 h for better dye absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of counter electrode and assembling of DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: Commercially available platisol was coated by doctor blade technique on FTO and sintered at 450 °C for 30 min to obtain platinum counter electrode. Further, the prepared photoanode and counter electrode were sandwiched, the Iodolyte electrolyte was dropped, and finally, the DSSC was assembled. The area (0.25 cm2) of the photoanode was kept constant throughout the study.

XRD data was recorded using a high-resolution Rigaku miniflex powder X-ray diffractometer with CuKα, a radiation source of wavelength (1.540 Å). Raman spectrum was recorded using the Renishaw model with a 532 nm excitation wavelength laser. UV absorption and diffuse reflectance studies were carried out using Shimadzu UV2600i, and PL fluorescence was observed by (Horiba Duetta fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained from, AXIS Supra instrumentation. Scanning electron micrographs were obtained from Apreo S, Thermo Fisher Scientific, with an acceleration voltage of 30 kV. A spin coater from Navson Technologies was used for spin coating GQDs. J-V measurements were carried out using a Photo Emission Tech (PET) solar simulator (SS50AAA-EM) with an illumination of 100 mWcm−2 and an optical filter of AM1.5G with Keithley source meter model no. 2401. IPCE measurements were done using Enlitech QE-R. All the electrochemical analyses were carried out using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation.

The crystal phase of the synthesized and fabricated samples was studied using XRD analysis. Fig. 1 a represents the XRD pattern of bare T and TAG. The films exhibit a major anatase phase with a trace of the Brookite phase. The diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 25.42°, 37.02°, 37.80°, 38.64°, 48.13°, 54.0°, 55.13°, 62.73°, 68.88°, 70.33°, and 75.13° which corresponds to [101], [103], [004], [112], [200], [105], [211], [204], [116], [220], and [215] crystallographic planes, respectively. Traces of the brookite phase were also observed with a peak at 30.93° with a smaller intensity corresponding to [121] crystallographic phase. Ag content in the films could not be distinguished, as the Ag peaks at [111] and [200] planes overlap with TiO2 peaks at [004] and [215] planes [35].

Raman analysis of GQD, as shown in Fig. 1b, exhibits two characteristic bands located at 1388 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1, corresponding to the D band and G band, respectively. The G band mainly originates from the zone center E2g mode, corresponding to in-plane vibrations of the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and the D band is ascribed to edges, defects and disordered carbon and arises from the breathing modes of sp2 rings [36]. From Fig. 1c, it has been observed that the samples exhibited anatase vibration modes at 144, 196 and 639 cm−1 corresponding to Eg, 398 cm−1 corresponds to B1g, and 517 cm−1 corresponds to A1g + B1g. In addition, a peak at 320 cm−1 corresponding to brookite vibration modes of B1g was also observed [37]. Beyond the modes of anatase and brookite phase, a small bump around 100 cm−1 was observed, which could have occurred due to the silver environment due to metallic clusters [38]. Further, the presence of the D band and G band in TAG-based film highlights the strong interaction between the TiO2 and GQD. This further confirms that GQD's presence influences the conduction of charge carriers through the metal oxide semiconductor. In addition, the introduction of GQD and Ag caused a decrease in the Raman intensity (Fig. 1d), which could be due to lattice distortions and changes in the electronic environment [39].

FESEM and HRTEM techniques were used to analyze the morphology of the synthesized TiO2 and graphene quantum dots, respectively. The FESEM images (Fig. 2 a) show the formation of TiO2 nanoparticles with the traces of Ag and GQD. The SEM images depict the cross-sectional view of the bare (T) and modified photoanodes (TAG) as shown in Fig. 2b and (c). The thickness of the fabricated photoanode is found to be approximately 9.1 μm. Further elemental mapping (Fig. 2d) confirms the presence of carbon from the GQD, Ag, Ti and O on the fabricated TAG film.

Fig. 3 a shows the transmission electron microscopy images of GQDs derived from anthracite. The as-synthesized GQDs had a uniform dispersion with homogenous size and shape. An average particle size of 7 nm was obtained. Also, the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3b and (c)) indicates that the GQDs are crystalline with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to the hexagonal lattice fringes of graphene [40].

The optical properties of the synthesized GQD were studied using absorbance and emission spectra. The GQDs exhibited a broad absorbance spanning the visible and UV regions. Fig. 4 a shows an absorption peak at 319 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition of CO, commonly reported in graphene systems. Several tiny bumps ranging between 340 and 450 nm were detected. The bump at 346 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of the functional groups present in the GQDs, while a bump at 364 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition. In addition, tiny peaks at 385 and 433 nm corresponding to the n-π* transition were also observed. The presence of carboxylic acid functional groups in the GQDs results in these n-π* transition peaks [41,42]. A PL emission at 485 nm was obtained when the GQDs were excited with an excitation wavelength of 270 nm. With the excitation in the UV region, the GQDs were seen to emit light in the visible region, which showed down-conversion behavior in the synthesized GQDs. Further, the optical properties of the fabricated T, TA, TG and TAG films were studied using diffuse reflectance spectra. Bare TiO2 exhibited absorption only in the UV region as their characteristic property. But with the inclusion of Ag and GQD the absorbance was extended to the visible region. This wider absorption can be attributed to the surface plasmon resonance contributed by the silver particles and enhancement in intensity could be attributed to the absorbance and down-conversion properties exhibited by the GQDs [16]. The emitted visible light from the electron down conversion materials (GQD) is absorbed by Ag particles and N719 dye, resulting in high photogenerated electron density, rapid charge carrier generation and transportation, ultimately leading to enhanced current density and better photon harvesting ability in DSSC. Fig. 4b shows the UV–visible DRS plot of T, TG, TA and TAG-based films. Bare TiO2(T) showed an absorption in the UV region based on its inherent property. Further, with the addition of GQD, there could be an increase in the absorption observed in the visible region, which could be mainly attributed to the π-π and Vander wall's force present between the TiO2 and the GQD. With the inclusion of the Ag layer, an extended absorption in the visible region is seen, which is mainly due to the surface plasmon effect of the silver particles caused by the collective oscillation of the electron in the noble silver particles. Furthermore, the broadening of the absorption region with the incorporation of the graphene quantum dots would be mainly due to the better electron transport due to the auxiliary electron's presence which is further supported by the reflectance plot as shown in Fig. 4c [10,17].

The elemental composition of bare TiO2 and GQD + Ag + TiO2 film was analyzed from the XPS spectrum, as shown in Fig. (5a and 5b) . Fig. 5a shows the Ti and O peaks in the survey scan of bare TiO2, whereas Fig. 5b shows four peaks of Ti-2p, O-1s, Ag-3d and C1s elements present in the GQD + Ag + TiO2. The high-resolution Ti-2p spectra of TiO2 present in GQD + Ag + TiO2 film, as shown in Fig. 5c, exhibited two peaks centered at binding energies 459.5 and 465.2 eV corresponding to Ti-2p3/2 and Ti-2p1/2, indicating the presence of Ti4+ oxidation state. Fig. 5d shows Ag 5d core level spectra, in which two peaks at 366.9 and 373.0 eV corresponded to binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 core levels owing to the presence of metallic silver [43]. The presence of carbon C1s peak confirms the presence of GQD. A broad peak with three peaks, when deconvoluted in the C1s spectra, links to different forms of carbon and confirms the presence of functional groups, as shown in Fig. 5f. The dominant peak at binding energies of 284.5 eV shows that the GQDs are mainly composed of sp2 graphitic carbon. Further peaks at 286.0 and 288.3 eV correspond to C–O (alcoholic carbon) and –COOH (carboxyl) functional groups [44]. The presence of functional groups aids in better linking of dye and TiO2 nanoparticles and enhances the faster transfer of excited electrons. Further, Fig. 5 (e) shows the O1s spectra, in which the peak at 530.7 eV corresponds to the binding energy of oxygen with TiO2, and the peak at 532.6 eV corresponds to the CO bonding.

The J-V measurements were carried out to study the effect of GQD on the photovoltaic performance of plasmonic DSSC, shown in Fig. 6 a. Corresponding device photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . All the fabricated DSSCs were tested under 100mWcm−2 of simulated solar AM 1.5G irradiation. The pristine DSSC (T) exhibited an efficiency of 7.30 % with Jsc, Voc, and FF values of 16.18 mAcm−2, 0.74 V and 0.61. An efficiency of 9.20 % with a current density of 20.66 mAcm−2, open circuit voltage of 0.77 V, and 0.58 as a fill factor for the DSSC with GQD as a co-sensitizer (TG). Further, in comparison, the plasmonics DSSC (TA) exhibited an efficiency of 9.30 % with Jsc, Voc, and FF values of 20.72 mAcm−2, 0.77 V and 0.58. It can be observed that with the inclusion of GQD, there is a gradual improvement in the cell's performance with an efficiency of 10.50 % and having Jsc, Voc, and FF values of 22.40 mAcm−2, 0.76 V and 0.62, respectively. The co-sensitizing characteristic of GQD and silver particles' surface plasmon resonance effect broadens the absorption window (as seen in Fig. 4b) and enhances the optical absorbance. These characteristics collaborate to improve light harvesting and thus boost the cell's overall efficiency.

The repeatability of data was confirmed by fabricating multiple devices, and their photovoltaic parameters, such as Jsc, Voc, fill factor, and efficiency, were recorded, as shown in Fig. S1 (a)–(d). On comparing the pristine device (T) with the (TAG) cell, as seen in Fig.S1 (a-d), it could be observed that the TAG device showed higher current density, an appreciable increase in open circuit voltage and comparable fill factor. Additionally, nanocarbon (TAG device) consistently demonstrated the highest efficiency at 10.50 %. The shelf life of the fabricated DSSCs (Fig. S1 (e)) was examined. After 48 h, a decrement in the performance was observed, mainly due to the electrolyte leakage and not sealing the DSSC. The IPCE spectra of T, TA, TG and TAG-based DSSC are presented in Fig. 6b. As observed from the graph, the spectral response increases with the inclusion of GQD, which agrees with the obtained Jsc values as observed from the J-V curves. This enhancement in the IPCE can be attributed to the improved light absorption in the DSSC.

Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy has been widely used to understand the internal charge resistance and transfer process in a DSSC. As seen from Fig. 6c, the Nyquist diagrams of the solar cells measured with a bias voltage of 0.75 V in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 KHz under dark conditions showed the semicircles corresponding to the charge transfer at the photoanode/electrolyte interface and platinum/electrolyte interface. In comparison, it could be understood that the TAG-based cell has a smaller semicircle radius than the other cells. The decreased radius reveals the lowest charge transfer resistance of 3.76 Ω for (TAG) GQD + Ag + TiO2 based DSSC in comparison to the pristine TiO2 device, which exhibited a charge transfer resistance of 11.14 Ω for (T). Further, from the Bode phase plots (Fig. 6d), it was determined that the injected electron recombination lifetime using the formula of τCB = 1/2πf in which τCB is the electron lifetime in TiO2 and ƒ is the peak frequency from bode plots. It was observed that the addition of silver plasmonics and GQD layer on the photoanode facilitated a better electron transportation path with the fastest electron time of 12.4 ms in comparison to the pristine device, which exhibited 34.2 ms. The decrease in the Rct value and the swift transfer of electrons in the TAG-based device can be attributed to the better charge transfer ability of GQD by acting as a passivation layer and thus reducing the charge recombination in the fabricated devices.

To attain more insight into the photophysical origin of PCE and understand the charge dynamics, the films' photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded over a range of 325–500 nm. Fig. 7 a shows a comparative PL spectrum of T, TA, TG and TAG-based films. The PL emission spectra were obtained for an excitation wavelength of 275 nm and emission peaks at around 400 nm and 420 nm, which correspond to the surface state emission and the oxygen defects present in the sample. An intense quenching was observed with the inclusion of Ag particles and GQD. This decrease in the PL intensity indicated that there is good separation of the electron-hole pairs and long-lived charge carriers, and it can also be attributed to the graphitic content present in the GQD, which facilitates a better charge transfer at the TiO2/(GQD + Ag)/N719 interface, thus indicating the reduced surface charge trapping.

Open circuit voltage decay was used to understand the charge recombination and charge transport efficiency at the photoanode/electrolyte interface in the fabricated DSSCs. The measurements were done by illuminating the cells with a 40 W incandescent lamp for 10s to a steady voltage, and then the illumination was turned off. The readings were recorded using an electrochemical workstation and taken for a duration of 60 s. Fig. 7b shows a constant photovoltage in the order TAG > TA > TG > T was observed. This suggests an increased electron concentration in TAG in comparison with pristine T-based DSSC. A decay process in the voltage is observed when the light is switched off, showcasing the recombination process of electrons in the conduction band of TiO2 and electrolyte. A slower decay profile is exhibited by the TAG-based DSSC, suggesting a decrease in the interfacial recombination compared to the other DSSCs.

Conventionally, Voc is mainly defined as the voltage difference between the redox potential of the electrolyte and the quasi-fermi potential of the semiconductor. As the redox potential of the I−/I3 − is constant for the fabricated DSSCs, the variation in the open circuit voltage would be mainly contributed by the potential of the conduction band edge of the semiconductor, which is related to the surface charge and chargerecombination. On comparing the bare TiO2 (T) with TAG-based DSSC, an increase in the Voc of 20 mV was observed (Fig. 8 b). The reason for this increment in the open circuit voltage was analyzed using Mott-Schottky studies of the impedance spectra. This study carried out the semiconductor electrode measurements' flat band potential (Vfb), and a negative shift of Vfb compared to the bare one (T) was observed. The shift in the fermi level can be attributed to the charge localization, which is caused by the decreased work function of the TiO2 due to the plasmonics effect and the role of GQD as a co-sensitizer.

Further, the GQD/Ag plasmonic-assisted photoanode was subjected to photoelectrochemical measurements using a three-electrode system and linear-sweep voltammetry (LSV), revealing a significant enhancement in photocurrent, as seen in Fig. 8a. The working electrode in this setup is the GQD/Ag plasmonic-assisted TiO2 photoanode film, which is placed on the FTO substrate. Pt serves as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl functions as the reference electrode. The figure showcases the relationship between photocurrent and potential, measured in a 0.1 M NaOH electrolyte. The analysis of Fig. 8a reveals that all films displayed an anodic photocurrent. Notably, the GQD/Ag plasmonic-assisted photoanode film (TAG) exhibited higher photocurrent values compared to the bare TiO2 film. This distinction can be attributed to the GQD/Ag plasmonic-induced photocurrent mechanism, wherein hot electrons, propelled by the surface plasmon-induced charge separation of Ag plasmon, are injected into the conduction band of TiO2 via GQD. Consequently, the generation of GQD/Ag plasmon-assisted photocurrent is observed. The heightened current density is likely a result of multiple factors working together: the resonance of silver nanoparticles enhancing light absorption and scattering, along with the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) facilitating rapid charge transfer. This is illustrated in the provided diagram (see Fig. 9 ). The surplus photocurrent appears to be generated through a direct transfer of electrons induced by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) from the core-shell structure of the silver nanoparticles to the conduction band of TiO2. A comparison table of various studies corresponding to silver and gold plasmonic with nanocarbon and their corresponding J-V data have been provided in Table S1 (supplementary information).

In conclusion, the fabricated TAG-based DSSC showed a synergistic effect of anthracite-derived GQD and silver plasmonics by exhibiting an enhanced power conversion efficiency of 10.50 % when illuminated with solar irradiation of 100 mWcm−2 with AM1.5G. A facile technique comprising a processable photochemical solution was employed for the fabrication of silver plasmons on the photoanodes of DSSC, and further GQDs were spin-coated to form a passivation layer on the as-prepared photoanode. The combined effect of lowest charge transfer resistance as observed from the EIS studies, surface plasmon resonance exhibited by silver particles, enhanced absorption in the visible region and surface passivation due to the presence of graphene quantum dots led to improved performance of DSSC. Thus, the results of this study provide novel avenues for the utilization of anthracite-derived GQD in the realm of sustainable energy conversion devices.

Akshatha A. Rao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Santhosh Narendhiran: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Manoj Balachandran: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge DST for major research fund DST/TMD/SERI/ARSS/2020/37(G), Centre for Research Christ (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221. The authors also acknowledge the central instrumentation facility (CIF) Christ (Deemed to be University) for the instrument facility. We also thank DST-FIST for their support through the research fund SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). Authors are also grateful to Central Research Facilities, IIT Delhi, Sonipat campus for their support with XPS characterisation. Akshatha A Rao also acknowledges UGC for the research fund obtained from SJSGC Fellowship. Grant number: UGCES-22-GE-KAR-F-SJSGC-3769.
